note: Curated qualitative overlay, researched 2026-07-07 by parallel Claude research agents with live web search; every entry independently fact-checked by an adversarial verifier agent (corrections folded in, marked [Verifier: ...]). Qualitative facts go stale - check the per-country sources before acting.
bondsAccessNote: Individual EM USD sovereign bonds trade over-the-counter, and a US retail investor can only realistically buy them through brokers with a real bond desk (Interactive Brokers has the widest EM inventory; Fidelity and Schwab carry a thinner, mostly SEC-registered selection). Minimums are the killer: some SEC-registered Latin sovereigns (Mexico, Brazil, Colombia) come in $1,000 pieces, but many frontier eurobonds (Nigeria, Ghana, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Egypt) are Reg S/144A issues with $200,000 minimum denominations, which locks retail out of exactly the credits this dashboard cares about. Even where you can buy, retail fills routinely cost 1-3% in spread/markup versus institutional prices, and selling before maturity in a stressed name can be much worse. For anyone below roughly mid-six figures per position, EMB (0.39% ER, ~$14.3B, penny spreads) or EMHY (0.50%, holds the high-yield/distressed sovereigns) is the honest answer: one-share minimum, daily liquidity, and diversified default risk, at the cost of never holding a single country's bond to maturity. Verified both funds actively trading as of 2026-07-07.

reformer.name: Javier Milei (president), with Luis Caputo as Economy Minister
reformer.role: President (La Libertad Avanza); Caputo runs the economic program
reformer.nextElection: General/presidential election October 2027 (Milei eligible for re-election); last national vote was the 26 Oct 2025 midterms
reformer.notes: Milei is the benchmark case for this thesis. Oct 2025 midterms delivered a landslide: LLA won 40.84% nationally vs 31.63% for Peronist Fuerza Patria, adding ~64 Deputies and 14 Senators - enough for veto power though still short of an outright majority. Program execution since: labor reform passed Senate 42-28 (Feb 2026), EU-Mercosur ratified, ~90 structural-reform bills announced for 2026, three consecutive years of fiscal surplus targeted, and on 7 Jul 2026 Caputo presented a financing program through 2027 with an explicit 'investment grade by 2031' goal. Docked points because Congress majority is still lacking (reforms need allies) and disinflation has stalled ~33% YoY, with PIIE flagging the inflation-indexed FX band as a weak nominal anchor. No credible political threat to the program before Oct 2027.

parallelFx.notes: Gap has effectively collapsed - a residual blue (informal) market exists but is described as 'negligible and a non-issue'. bluedollar.net quotes for 7 Jul 2026: blue 1,495 buy / 1,515 sell; official 1,461.14 buy / 1,513.07 sell (retail). Rates above are midpoints; gap is ~2.3% on the buy side and near 0% on the sell side (guides cite 2-5% under Milei vs 50-100% historically). Most capital controls (cepo) were lifted 14 Apr 2025 with the $20bn IMF deal; regime is a managed float inside a band. Since 1 Jan 2026 the band's floor/ceiling crawl monthly at the INDEC inflation rate with a 2-month lag (replacing the 1%/month crawl), paired with a pre-announced BCRA reserve-buying program (~5% of daily FX volume). Caveat: PIIE (Feb 2026) notes the peso trades near the band's upper limit, leaving little buffer against shocks. [Verifier: 2.3% was a buy-side-only comparison; midpoint-vs-midpoint is ~1.2% - curated as 1.5%.]

countryRisk.rating: Moody's Caa1 stable (upgraded from Caa3, 17 Jul 2025); Fitch B- stable (upgraded from CCC+, 5 May 2026); S&P B- stable (upgraded two notches from CCC+, 10 Jun 2026)
countryRisk.notes: JP Morgan EMBI 'riesgo pais' closed at 408bps on 6-7 Jul 2026 - the lowest of the Milei era and the lowest since 24 Apr 2018 - after the government presented its 2026-27 financing program (vs >2,000bps before Milei's 2023 election, ~514bps in early May 2026, 431bps on 29 Jun 2026). Two agencies upgraded within roughly a month of each other in mid-2026; S&P cited easing external vulnerabilities and a ~$10bn 2026 energy trade surplus from Vaca Muerta. Fitch says further upgrades hinge on sustained FX reserve build-up (BCRA had bought ~US$3.5bn in 2026 against a US$10-17bn goal).

ARG.imfProgram: Active 48-month Extended Fund Facility of about US$21bn (SDR 15.267bn, 479% of quota), approved 11 April 2025. Second review completed by the Executive Board on 21 May 2026 together with the 2026 Article IV consultation, unlocking ~US$1bn and bringing total disbursements to ~US$15.8bn. Board assessed implementation as strong; the end-Dec 2025 net international reserves (NIR) accumulation target was missed but most other performance criteria were met and corrective measures are in place.

latestInflation.asOf: May 2026 (INDEC, released 11 Jun 2026; June print due mid-July)
latestInflation.notes: Disinflation has STALLED in the low-30s rather than continuing to fall: m/m path was Jan 2.9%, Feb 2.9%, Mar 3.4% (utility/transport tariff adjustments), Apr 2.8%, May 2.1%; YoY was 32.4% Jan, 33.1% Feb, ~32.6% Mar, 32.4% Apr, 33.2% May (Trading Economics shows 33.6% for May - minor source discrepancy; 33.2% is the INDEC report figure per IndexBox). Cumulative Jan-May 2026: 14.7%. The re-acceleration vs late 2025 reflects peso depreciation passthrough and regulated-price catch-up; the May m/m easing to 2.1% is encouraging but PIIE warns the inflation-indexed band mechanically accommodates whatever inflation the economy produces.

ARG.fiscalNote: In surplus and holding: primary surplus of ~0.5% of GDP and financial (overall) surplus of ~0.2% of GDP cumulated in Jan-Apr 2026, after full-year fiscal surpluses in 2024 and 2025; the 2026 IMF-agreed target is a 1.6%-of-GDP primary surplus, which the government says would make three consecutive years of financial surplus. Trajectory is positive but softening at the margin: April 2026 primary surplus (AR$632bn, ~US$452m) was down 16.5% y/y in real terms and tax revenue fell ~4% real y/y, driven by a 34.4% real collapse in commodity export-duty receipts - spending discipline must hold through December to hit the target.
developments: Milei's LLA won the 26 Oct 2025 midterms with 40.84% nationally (Peronists 31.63%), gaining ~64 lower-house seats and 14 senators - securing veto power and a reform mandate through 2027 [source: https://buenosairesherald.com/politics/argentina-2025-midterms-lla-gets-landslide-win-reaches-key-number-of-congress-seats]

US Treasury's US$20bn currency swap (announced 9 Oct 2025) stabilized the peso pre-election; Argentina drew US$2.5bn and fully repaid it by 9 Jan 2026, per Treasury Secretary Bessent [source: https://fortune.com/2026/01/09/argentina-has-repaid-its-20-billion-credit-line-from-trump-administration-scott-bessent-says/]

From 1 Jan 2026 the FX band crawls monthly at the INDEC inflation rate (t-2 lag) instead of 1%/month, alongside a pre-announced BCRA reserve-buying program of ~5% of daily FX market volume [source: https://buenosairesherald.com/economics/argentina-to-adjust-currency-bands-by-inflation-and-accumulate-reserves]

Senate passed Milei's labor reform 42-28 on 28 Feb 2026 (12-hour workday option, relaxed hiring rules, salaries payable in foreign currency); EU-Mercosur agreement ratified 69-3 [source: https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/28/argentina-senate-approves-contentious-milei-backed-labour-reforms]

Fitch upgraded Argentina to B- (stable) from CCC+ on 5 May 2026 - first Fitch upgrade in eight years - citing structurally improved fiscal and external balances [source: https://www.tradingview.com/news/reuters.com,2026:newsml_FWN41I1BZ:0-fitch-upgrades-argentina-to-b-outlook-stable/]

IMF Board completed the second EFF review on 21 May 2026, disbursing ~US$1bn (total ~US$15.8bn of ~US$21bn); implementation judged strong though the end-Dec NIR target was missed [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/05/21/pr26165-argentina-imf-completes-2nd-rev-of-extended-arr-under-eff-concludes-2026-aiv-consultation]

S&P upgraded Argentina two notches to B- (stable) from CCC+ on 10 Jun 2026, citing easing vulnerabilities and a projected ~US$10bn 2026 energy trade surplus from Vaca Muerta [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-10/argentina-rating-raised-to-b-by-s-p-in-win-for-milei]

On 7 Jul 2026 Caputo presented a financing program through 2027 (new Bonar 2029, no international issuance needed in base case, 'investment grade by 2031' goal); riesgo pais fell to 408bps, lowest since Apr 2018 [source: https://www.infobae.com/economia/2026/07/07/el-riesgo-pais-toco-el-nivel-mas-bajo-en-la-era-milei-tras-la-presentacion-del-programa-financiero/]
killCriteriaWatch: None of the four kill criteria is tripping as of 2026-07-07. Reformer in power: SAFE (midterm landslide, no election until Oct 2027). Country risk: SAFE and improving (408bps, 8-year low). Parallel gap: SAFE (~0-2.3%).

Closest watch item - fiscal softening: April 2026 primary surplus fell 16.5% y/y real and tax revenue fell ~4% real (export duties -34.4% real); surplus is intact (0.5% GDP primary Jan-Apr) but the 1.6% full-year target requires discipline through December. Not tripped, monitor monthly Treasury results.

Second watch item - disinflation stalled and FX framework fragility: YoY inflation is stuck at ~32-34% (m/m re-accelerated to 2.9-3.4% in Q1 2026 before easing to 2.1% in May), and PIIE warns the inflation-indexed band lacks a nominal anchor with the peso near the band ceiling - an adverse shock could force a devaluation and reopen the parallel gap (the autumn-2025 episode shows the mechanism). Early-warning, not tripped.

Third watch item - reserves: end-Dec 2025 IMF NIR target was missed (corrective measures adopted); Fitch says further re-rating hinges on reserve build-up (~US$3.5bn bought in 2026 vs US$10-17bn goal). A stall in reserve accumulation would precede any FX/gap trouble.

access.accessNotes: ARGT is liquid (~$826.6M AUM, last close $92.81 - ETF access check 2026-07-07). Deep NYSE ADR bench too (YPF, GGAL, BMA, PAM, VIST) for single-name expression.

reformer.name: Daniel Noboa (president); Sariha Moya (economy/finance chief)
reformer.role: Noboa: President since Nov 2023, re-elected April 2025. Moya: Minister of Economy and Finance since 16 Apr 2025; since the June 2026 cabinet consolidation she heads the merged Ministry of Economic Development and Production (finance + agriculture + production).
reformer.nextElection: General (presidential/legislative) election due Feb 2029; Noboa's term runs to May 2029. No national vote scheduled before then after the Nov 16, 2025 referendum.
reformer.notes: Serious, IMF-anchored orthodox program rather than Milei-style shock therapy: diesel subsidy removed Sept 2025 ($1.80->$2.80/gal, ~$1.1bn/yr savings) despite a 31-day indigenous-led national strike; tax-expenditure streamlining and corrective fiscal package after late-2025 slippage; return to bond markets Jan 2026. Constraints on the score: voters rejected his constituent-assembly rewrite 61.7% in the Nov 2025 referendum (Fitch flagged governability concerns), record-high homicides, conflicts with the Constitutional Court, and a 2025 fiscal overshoot. Re-election in April 2025 gives him a mandate through 2029, so program continuity risk is moderate.

parallelFx.notes: Not applicable: Ecuador has been fully dollarized since 9 Jan 2000 (sucre abolished under Mahuad); the US dollar is legal tender, so there is no own-currency exchange rate and no parallel FX market. Polling cited at the 25-year anniversary (Jan 2025) shows ~90% public support for dollarization; de-dollarization is not on any major party's agenda. Rates set to null because no ECU exchange rate exists.

countryRisk.rating: Moody's Caa1 stable (upgraded from Caa3, 27 Jan 2026); Fitch B- stable (upgraded from CCC+, 26 Feb 2026); S&P B- stable (affirmed 20 Aug 2025, outlook revised to stable from negative)
countryRisk.notes: EMBI/riesgo pais was 378 bps on 15 Jun 2026 per BCE data reported by El Universo - the lowest since Sept 2014 - vs 1,908 bps in April 2025, 855 bps in June 2025 and 492 at end-2025 (down ~114 bps YTD). A July 2026 print was not findable; 378 is the latest verified reading (~3 weeks old). Compression drivers: IMF program performance, Jan 2026 $4bn market return ($18bn book, 4.5x oversubscribed) with $3bn buyback of 2030s/2035s cutting debt service ~$1.2bn through 2028, record reserves (~$11.9bn), and high oil prices (Brent ~$110-114). Ecuador USD bonds ~+5% YTD in May 2026, among the strongest EM performers.

ECU.imfProgram: 48-month Extended Fund Facility of SDR 3.75bn (~US$5bn), approved May 2024 and augmented July 2025. Fifth review completed by the Executive Board on 22 Apr 2026 (staff-level agreement 31 Mar 2026), unlocking an immediate disbursement of ~US$400m; review included waivers of nonobservance of performance criteria after the late-2025 fiscal overshoot, with corrective revenue/spending measures enacted. Moody's (Jan 2026) put cumulative disbursements at ~US$3.3bn.

latestInflation.notes: May 2026: 0.92% YoY, -0.62% m/m (INEC data via Trading Economics), down from 2.60% YoY / +0.53% m/m in April 2026 and 2.33% in March. Inflation is structurally low under dollarization (2025 average ~1.5%); the April spike (housing/utilities +17.3%) partly reflects the Sept 2025 diesel-subsidy pass-through. IMF projects 2.0-2.8% for 2026. June 2026 print not yet published/indexed at time of research (INEC releases ~4th business day of month). Inflation is NOT the problem in this country - the thesis leg here is fiscal + spreads, not disinflation.

ECU.fiscalNote: Still in deficit but consolidating under the IMF program, with a late-2025 slippage now being corrected. IMF 5th review (Apr 2026): NFPS overall deficit was 2.9% of GDP in 2025, overshooting the 4th-review projection by 1.7pp of GDP; corrective measures (streamlined tax expenditures, revenue strengthening, spending efficiency) enacted to return to a path targeting ~6.6% of GDP non-oil primary consolidation over the program, with ~1.6% of GDP of additional non-oil primary consolidation in 2026. Moody's (Jan 2026): central government deficit narrowed to ~2.3% of GDP in 2025 from 2.8% in 2024, forecast ~1.8% in 2026; NFPS deposits at the central bank at a record $5.8bn (4.5% of GDP) as of Oct 2025. Caveat: perimeters differ - Rio Times reports the central government cash deficit widened to $5.3bn in 2025 from $3.1bn in 2024, consistent with the IMF's flagged underperformance; 2025 interest bill $4.1bn, total 2026 debt service ~$12.8bn. Current account in sizable surplus; reserves at record ~$11.9bn (Feb-Mar 2026).
developments: Jan 26, 2026: Ecuador returned to international bond markets for the first time since 2019, selling $4bn ($2.2bn 2034s at 8.75%, $1.8bn 2039s at 9.25%) on an $18bn, 4.5x-oversubscribed book, and used $3bn to buy back ~80% of the 2030s and ~30% of the 2035s, cutting debt service ~$1.2bn through 2028 [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-26/ecuador-tapping-debt-markets-for-first-time-since-restructuring]

Jan 27, 2026: Moody's upgraded Ecuador two notches to Caa1 (stable) from Caa3, citing durable fiscal policy changes under the IMF program and restored market access [source: https://www.investing.com/news/world-news/moodys-upgrades-ecuador-to-caa1-from-caa3-maintains-stable-outlook-93CH-4468238]

Feb 26, 2026: Fitch upgraded Ecuador to B- (stable) from CCC+, citing reduced state financing needs, a historic current-account surplus and sustained reserve accumulation [source: https://www.eloriente.com/articulo/fitch-ratings-mejora-la-calificacion-de-ecuador-y-mantiene-perspectiva-estable/55614]

Apr 22, 2026: IMF Executive Board completed the fifth EFF review, disbursing ~$400m, granting waivers for the late-2025 fiscal overshoot (2025 NFPS deficit 2.9% of GDP, 1.7pp above projection) against enacted corrective measures [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/04/22/pr26126-ecuador-the-imf-concludes-5th-review-of-eff-arrangement]

May 5-6, 2026: Second 2026 bond operation - ~$1bn re-tap of the 2034s/2039s at ~8.975% average yield, with country risk ~485bps and Brent ~$114 [source: https://www.riotimesonline.com/ecuador-second-sovereign-bond-issuance-2034-2039-may-2026/]

Jun 15, 2026: Riesgo pais fell to 378 bps, the lowest since September 2014, down from 1,908 bps in April 2025 and 2,016 bps when Noboa took office in Nov 2023 [source: https://www.eluniverso.com/noticias/economia/riesgo-pais-sigue-bajando-378-puntos-nuevo-record-banco-central-del-ecuador-2026-nota/]

Nov 16, 2025 (context for 2026 politics): Voters rejected Noboa's referendum - 61.7% against a constituent assembly, 60.8% against foreign military bases - his first major electoral defeat, which Fitch said raised governability concerns; a 31-day indigenous-led national strike had earlier protested the diesel-subsidy elimination [source: https://constitutionnet.org/news/voters-ecuadors-referendum-reject-proposal-constituent-assembly]

H1 2026: Agreement on Reciprocal Trade with the US eliminates the 15% surcharge on ~half of non-oil exports (~$3.2bn); reserves hit a record ~$11.86bn in Feb 2026; 2025 GDP grew 3.7% with shrimp overtaking crude as the top export at a record $8.4bn [source: https://www.riotimesonline.com/ecuador-economy-2026-gdp-growth-oil-crisis-export-surge-and-the-noboa-reform-agenda/]
killCriteriaWatch: Budget flipping back to deficit - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING (technically Ecuador never reached surplus): the 2025 NFPS deficit of 2.9% of GDP overshot the IMF program projection by 1.7pp, forcing waivers at the 5th review; corrective measures are enacted but the 2026 consolidation (~1.6% of GDP non-oil primary) must be delivered, and fiscal accounts are heavily flattered by Brent at ~$110-114 - an oil-price drop is the main channel for renewed slippage.

Reformer losing power - WATCH, not tripped: Noboa is secure in office until 2029, but the Nov 2025 referendum defeat (61.7% No), a 31-day indigenous-led strike against the diesel-subsidy cut, record homicides, and clashes with the Constitutional Court show eroding political capital; further subsidy/tax measures face street-level veto risk (Ecuador's Oct 2019 and Jun 2022 precedents of reversals under protest).
Country risk blowing out - NOT tripping: spreads at 378bps are 12-year lows and falling; the vulnerability is oil-price dependence and heavy 2026-2028 debt service (~$12.8bn in 2026).

Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening - NOT APPLICABLE: fully dollarized since 2000 with ~90% public support; no own currency exists to devalue. The dollarization-specific tail risk is a liquidity/deposit crisis rather than an FX gap, and record reserves (~$11.9bn) make that remote near-term.

access.accessNotes: No US-listed ETF and no meaningful ADRs. Realistic exposure is Ecuador USD sovereign bonds inside broad EM-debt funds (EMB/EMHY) - which dilutes the single-country thesis.

reformer.name: Abdel Fattah El-Sisi (with Finance Minister Ahmed Kouchouk and PM Mostafa Madbouly executing the program)
reformer.role: President; economic program executed by a technocratic team (Kouchouk at Finance, Hassan Abdalla at the central bank) under an IMF EFF anchor
reformer.nextElection: Presidential election due 2030 (Sisi's third term ends 2030; constitution currently bars re-election, but analysts report the new loyalist parliament could pass amendments extending his rule). Parliamentary elections were completed Nov-Dec 2025 with final results Jan 10, 2026.
reformer.notes: This is IMF-anchored orthodoxy driven by necessity, not Milei-grade ideological shock therapy. Delivered: March 2024 float and flexible FX regime maintained, fuel price hikes (+17% in March 2026 even mid-war), electricity tariff hikes, ~3.5% of GDP primary surplus, all March 2026 IMF quantitative performance criteria met, and a 2026 law signed by Sisi repealing Article 27 barriers to selling state companies (~60 SOEs slated, $10.3bn divestment target by FY2026/27). Constraint: IMF itself says structural reform is 'uneven' - reducing the state's (and military's) economic footprint lags badly, with critics noting little material divestment in 24 months while new military entities were created. Political risk to program continuity is low (authoritarian control, loyalist parliament with Nation's Future holding 227/568 seats); the risk is reform depth, not reversal. Score 55 = orthodox and durable but constrained by the state/military economy.

parallelFx.notes: Currency was floated/unified March 6, 2024 and the flexible regime has held; official USD/EGP was 48.81 on 2026-07-07 (TradingEconomics), with EGP up 6.22% over the past month after hitting a record-weak ~54.86 in March 2026 during the Iran war shock. A small parallel premium (~3-5%) was reported at the height of the war panic (Middle East Insider, March 2026, vs ~70% at the 2023-24 crisis peak), and earlier reporting (Asharq Al-Awsat) described the black-market gap shrinking to a few piasters post-float. No live, verifiable parallel quote for July 2026 could be fetched (black-market tracker sites sarfegp.com and egcurrency.com returned 403), so parallel_rate/gap_pct are null; all evidence points to no material parallel market as of 2026-07-07, with the pound appreciating.

countryRisk.rating: Moody's Caa1, positive outlook (affirmed April 2026; positive outlook in place since March 2024); S&P B/B, stable (upgraded to B from B- in late 2025, affirmed April 14, 2026); Fitch B, stable (affirmed per Arab News, late 2025)
countryRisk.notes: No verifiable current EMBI/USD-spread level in bps could be fetched (cbonds and worldgovernmentbonds pages inaccessible/unloaded), so spread_bps is null. Direction is clear: risk spiked with the 2026 Iran war (~$10bn foreign portfolio outflows within one month of conflict onset per S&P via EnterpriseAM; Moody's cited ~$8bn; Egypt detracted 18bps from EMBI GD returns in Q1 2026 per State Street), then recompressed after the April ceasefire - Egypt priced a $1bn 8-year USD social bond in May 2026 at 7.6% (tightened from 8% guidance on a $3.9bn book), its first USD issuance since the war began. CBE had reported eurobond yields falling ~66bps in Q3 2025 before the war. Reserves $52.8bn (March 2026, record) and banking-sector NFA ~$30bn support the trend.

EGY.imfProgram: Active. 46-month Extended Fund Facility approved Dec 16, 2022, extended through Dec 15, 2026, plus a Resilience and Sustainability Facility. IMF Executive Board completed the combined 5th and 6th EFF reviews and the 1st RSF review on Feb 26, 2026, unlocking ~US$2.3bn (~$2bn EFF + $273m RSF); cumulative EFF+RSF drawings ~US$5.2bn (190.7% of quota). All quantitative performance criteria for March 2026 reported met (primary surplus, tax revenue, divestment proceeds, social spending). IMF flags uneven progress on structural reform, especially reducing the state's economic footprint.

latestInflation.asOf: May 2026 (released ~June 10, 2026; June print not yet out as of 2026-07-07)
latestInflation.notes: Figures are urban headline CPI (CAPMAS): 14.6% YoY in May vs 14.9% April; m/m urban 1.6% (national headline 13.0% YoY, 1.4% m/m). Trend is falling from the March 2026 spike (~15.2%) but well above the January 2026 trough (11.9% per IMF; Feb was 13.4%) - the re-acceleration was driven by war-related fuel (+17% March), electricity and transport price hikes, not FX passthrough. CBE held its policy rate at 19% on May 21, 2026, warning fiscal adjustment measures and energy prices could slow disinflation toward its Q4 2026 target. For context, inflation was 24%+ in mid-2025, so the disinflation story is intact but was dented by the war.

EGY.fiscalNote: Primary surplus, overall deficit - improving primary trajectory, overall balance still negative and slipping vs target. First 9 months FY2025/26 (Jul 2025-Mar 2026): primary surplus EGP 748.8bn = 3.5% of GDP (vs 2.4% a year earlier), but overall budget deficit widened to 5.2% of GDP over the same 9 months (war costs, energy subsidies, heavy interest bill). FY2025/26 targets: primary surplus 4% of GDP (EGP 795bn), overall deficit ~7.3% of GDP. Moody's: interest payments peak in FY2026 at ~63% of general government revenue (~11% of GDP); S&P: general government debt falling to ~89% of GDP by June 2026 from 94% peak in 2023; gross financing needs still >40% of GDP.
developments: IMF Executive Board completed the 5th and 6th EFF reviews and 1st RSF review on Feb 26, 2026, unlocking ~$2.3bn and extending the EFF through Dec 15, 2026; growth revised up to 4.4% for FY2024/25 but structural reform progress called 'uneven' [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/02/26/pr-26064-egypt-imf-completes-5th-and-6th-revs-under-ext-arrange-under-eff-and-1st-rev-under-rsa]

The 2026 Iran war hit Egypt hard in Feb-Apr 2026: Israel suspended ~1.1 bcf/d of gas exports to Egypt (Israeli gas = 60% of Egypt's gas imports), Suez Canal Q1 2026 revenue fell ~38% YoY to ~$1.5bn on Houthi escalation, fuel prices were hiked 17% in March, and the pound slid to ~54.9/USD [source: https://themiddleeastinsider.com/2026/04/04/iran-war-impact-egypt-economy-5-numbers-2026/]

Roughly $10bn of foreign portfolio money exited Egypt within one month of the war's onset (S&P via EnterpriseAM); S&P nonetheless affirmed B/B stable on April 14, 2026, citing record reserves of $52.8bn and debt falling to ~89% of GDP [source: https://enterpriseam.com/egypt/2026/04/14/sp-global-affirms-egypts-rating-at-b-b-stable-outlook/]

Ceasefire took hold in early April 2026 and was formalized via the June 17 'Islamabad Memorandum' opening a 60-day window (to ~mid-Aug 2026) for a final US-Iran deal and Strait of Hormuz reopening - the key macro tail-risk gate for Egypt [source: https://www.axios.com/2026/06/14/us-iran-ceasefire-extended-hormuz-reopen-trump]

Egypt regained market access in May 2026 with a $1bn 8-year USD social bond priced at 7.6% (tightened from 8%) on a $3.9bn order book - its first international USD issuance since the war began [source: https://internationalfinance.com/markets/egypt-raises-usd-billion-dollar-denominated-social-bond-sale/]

Privatization push accelerated: Sisi signed a law repealing Article 27 obstacles to selling state-owned firms; ~60 SOEs targeted (40 to the Sovereign Fund, 20 for EGX listing) with ~$10.3bn in proceeds targeted by end-FY2026/27, and the Gabal El-Zeit deal signed with Alcazar Energy; all March 2026 IMF quantitative criteria met [source: https://manassa.news/en/news/26540]

CBE held its policy rate at 19% on May 21, 2026 after inflation re-accelerated (Feb 13.4% from 11.9% Jan on Ramadan food and education costs, then war-driven fuel/electricity hikes), pausing an easing cycle; May urban inflation eased to 14.6% [source: https://sis.gov.eg/en/media-center/news/cbe-keeps-key-interest-rates-unchanged-as-inflation-pressures-persist/]

Parliamentary elections concluded with final results Jan 10, 2026: pro-Sisi Nation's Future party won 227 of 568 elected seats on 32.4% turnout, with results annulled in ~47 districts over irregularities - cementing a loyalist legislature that analysts say could amend the constitution to extend Sisi's rule beyond 2030 [source: https://www.idea.int/democracytracker/report/egypt/november-2025]
killCriteriaWatch: Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening - PARTIALLY TRIPPED in Mar-Apr 2026 and then reversed: the Iran war pushed EGP to a record ~54.86/USD with a reported 3-5% parallel premium and ~$10bn portfolio outflows in a month; by July 7 the pound had recovered to 48.81 (+6.2% m/m) and no material parallel gap is evident. Trigger to watch: collapse of the 60-day Islamabad Memorandum ceasefire window expiring ~mid-August 2026.

Country risk blowing out - spiked in Q1 2026 (Egypt detracted 18bps from EMBI GD returns; $10bn outflows) but recompressed by May-June (7.6% new-issue yield, 3.9x book). Watch Suez Canal traffic (Q1 revenue -38% YoY) and Israeli gas flows (60% of gas imports still suspended per April reporting) - both are war-linked hard-currency channels.

Budget flipping back to deficit - the overall balance has ALWAYS been in deficit (5.2% of GDP in 9M FY25/26, wider than the full-year 7.3%-of-GDP glidepath implies is comfortable); the thesis-relevant metric, the primary surplus, is intact at 3.5% of GDP and rising, but energy subsidy costs and an interest bill peaking at ~63% of revenue in FY2026 are the slippage risks.

Reformer losing power - NOT close to tripping: Sisi is entrenched with a loyalist parliament through 2030 and the IMF program runs to Dec 2026; the real thesis risk is reform shallowness (state/military footprint barely shrinking, IMF calls progress 'uneven'), i.e., the re-rating stalls rather than reverses.

reformer.name: Abiy Ahmed (PM), with technocrats Mamo Mihretu (NBE Governor) and Ahmed Shide (Finance Minister)
reformer.role: Prime Minister driving the Homegrown Economic Reform (HGER) agenda; NBE governor and finance minister execute the FX/monetary/fiscal side
reformer.nextElection: General election held 1 June 2026 (Prosperity Party won landslide, 438 seats); next general election ~2031
reformer.notes: Program is serious and orthodox: July 2024 FX float, 15% policy rate with credit caps, IMF ECF with all quantitative performance criteria met through five reviews, banking sector opened to foreign banks (June 2025 directive), Ethiopian Securities Exchange launched Jan 2025, Ethio Telecom partial IPO, Eurobond restructuring near done. But it is gradualist state-led reform, not Milei-grade shock therapy: parallel FX market persists (~13-15% premium mid-2026), credit caps still in place, SOE strategy is 'reform not privatisation', and the Ethio Telecom IPO raised only ~10% of target. Political risk is NOT electoral (Abiy just won 438 seats in an election marred by opposition crackdown; no voting in Tigray) but conflict-driven: elevated risk of war with Eritrea/renewed Tigray conflict (Crisis Group 'powder keg', Feb 2026), plus reported ambitions to amend the constitution toward a presidential system. Score 65 = credible orthodox program with strong political control, docked for gradualism, persistent FX distortion, and war tail-risk. [Verifier: Prosperity Party won 438 of 501 contested seats (547-seat house; 46 constituencies did not vote due to conflict).]

parallelFx.notes: Birr was floated/unified July 2024 under the IMF program, but a parallel market re-emerged. Gap blew out to ~27% (official 137 vs parallel 174) by Aug 2025 (Daba Finance), nearly converged in Nov 2025 (parallel 180 vs forex-bureau 178.7, though NBE indicative was 155.7 = ~15% vs indicative), then re-widened. Mid-2026 readings: CBE official ~157.3 vs parallel ~178 = ~13.2% premium (capitalmarketethiopia.com); Capital Newspaper (20 Jun 2026): analysts estimate premium above 15%, NBE officials claim below 11%; Middle East war strengthened parallel demand. Official/parallel figures above are the mid-2026 capitalmarketethiopia quotes; treat gap as a 11-15%+ range, well above the NBE's 5-8% target.

countryRisk.rating: Moody's Caa3 stable (since Sep 2023); S&P foreign-currency SD (since Dec 2023), local-currency CCC+/C affirmed Mar 2025; Fitch foreign-currency Restricted Default, local-currency upgraded to CCC+ (Apr 2025)
countryRisk.notes: No clean current EMBI spread findable: Ethiopia is still in default on its sole $1bn Eurobond (missed Dec 2023 coupon), so spread is not meaningful; stale reference: EMBI/CDS ~6,000bps at Dec 2023 default. Market pricing signals sharply falling risk: the defaulted bond rallied to ~110 cents on the dollar (5 Jan 2026, Bloomberg) on the first agreement-in-principle. Restructuring path: Jan 2026 AIP (15% haircut) rejected by Official Creditor Committee 30 Jan 2026; talks collapsed 31 May 2026; new preliminary deal 29-30 June 2026 ($880m new bonds = 12% haircut, 6.15% coupon, amortizing 2026-2029, $99.375m past-due interest paid at settlement, tradable New Money Warrant), reportedly IMF-DSA-consistent with no objection from OCC co-chairs China/France; exchange offer expected in coming months. Official-creditor MoU on ~$8.4bn reached July 2025. Ratings should cure upward once the exchange completes; trend = risk falling but default status not yet exited.

ETH.imfProgram: Active 48-month ECF, SDR 2.556bn (~US$3.4bn, 850% of quota), approved 29 July 2024 supporting the HGER agenda. Fifth review completed 1 July 2026: US$464m (SDR 342.05m) disbursed, bringing total disbursements to ~US$2.65bn; all QPCs and most indicative targets met; ~US$200m rephased forward to cushion the Middle East war shock. Fourth review completed 16 January 2026 (US$261m). IMF welcomed the Eurobond agreement-in-principle and urged NBE readiness to tighten further if inflation pressures re-emerge.

latestInflation.notes: Trading Economics/CSA: May 2026 CPI +13.4% YoY (ten-month high) and +1.7% m/m, up from 11.7% in April, 9.4% in March, 9.7% in February - single-digit objective was met Dec 2025-Mar 2026 but inflation has since re-accelerated, driven by food (15% YoY, meat +19.5%) and Middle East war oil-price passthrough. NBE MPC (21 Mar 2026) held the policy rate at 15% and kept the 24% credit-growth cap, flagging upside risks; IMF (Jul 2026) projects average inflation falling from 16% (2024/25) to 11.7% (2025/26).

ETH.fiscalNote: Modest deficits, consolidating but set to widen slightly: IMF 5th review (Jul 2026) shows revenue mobilization rising to 10.8% of GDP in 2025/26 and public debt falling to 45.3% of GDP. Parliament ratified a 2.34 trillion birr (~$14.8bn) FY2026/27 budget in early July 2026 - spending +21% YoY with a deficit of 308.6bn birr = 2.36% of GDP (~1.4% excluding 214bn birr amortization), financed mostly by domestic bonds/T-bills. Fitch upgraded the local-currency rating partly on cut domestic borrowing. Not a surplus story like Argentina - a small, mostly domestically financed deficit with strong revenue growth.
developments: IMF completed the 5th ECF review on 1 Jul 2026: $464m disbursed (total ~$2.65bn), all quantitative performance criteria met, ~$200m brought forward to cushion the Middle East war shock [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/07/01/pr26235-ethiopia-imf-completes-5th-review-under-ecf-arrangement]

New preliminary Eurobond restructuring deal reached 29-30 Jun 2026: $880m new bonds (12% haircut), 6.15% coupon, amortizing Jul 2026-Jul 2029, $99.375m past-due interest paid at settlement, plus a tradable New Money Warrant; reportedly IMF-consistent with no OCC co-chair objection [source: https://www.ecofinagency.com/news-finances/3006-56913-ethiopia-and-bondholders-reach-new-preliminary-deal-on-1-billion-eurobond-restructuring]

Restructuring was rocky first: the Jan 2026 agreement-in-principle (15% haircut; bond rallied to ~110 cents) was rejected by the Official Creditor Committee on 30 Jan 2026 and talks collapsed 31 May 2026 before the June breakthrough [source: https://addisinsight.net/2026/01/30/ethiopia-pauses-1-bln-eurobond-restructuring-after-official-creditors-reject-terms/]

Abiy Ahmed's Prosperity Party won the 1 June 2026 general election in a landslide (438 seats, 94% claimed turnout) - but no voting in Tigray, 143 polling stations closed in Oromia/Amhara, and opposition crackdown concerns [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Ethiopian_general_election]

Inflation re-accelerated to 13.4% YoY in May 2026 (ten-month high) from 9.4% in March, on food prices and the Middle East war oil shock; NBE held its policy rate at 15% and kept the 24% credit cap on 21 Mar 2026 [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/ethiopia/inflation-cpi]

Parliament ratified a 2.34 trillion birr FY2026/27 budget (spending +21%) with the deficit widening to 2.36% of GDP, mostly domestically financed [source: https://www.fanamc.com/english/ethiopian-parliament-ratifies-2-34-trillion-birr-federal-budget-for-2026-27-fiscal-year/]

Parallel FX gap re-widened to ~13-15%+ by mid-2026 (analysts >15%, NBE claims <11%) despite the July 2024 float; NBE gave banks expanded FX approval authority to pull flows back into formal channels [source: https://capitalethiopia.com/2026/06/20/ethiopia-expects-birr-stability-in-2026-27-despite-heavy-fx-pressures/]

War risk elevated: Crisis Group (Feb 2026) calls Ethiopia-Eritrea-Tigray a 'powder keg', with Abiy's Red Sea port ambitions and alleged TPLF-Eritrea alignment raising odds of a new northern conflict [source: https://www.crisisgroup.org/brf/africa/ethiopia-eritrea/b210-ethiopia-eritrea-and-tigray-powder-keg-horn-africa]
killCriteriaWatch: Parallel gap reopening - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING: gap re-widened to ~13.2% (CBE 157.3 vs parallel 178) and analysts say >15% as of Jun 2026, vs near-convergence in late 2025 and an NBE target of 5-8%; it hit ~27% in Aug 2025, so the distortion is chronic

Inflation re-acceleration: 13.4% YoY May 2026, a ten-month high and rising for 3 straight months (Middle East war oil shock) - disinflation thesis is wobbling; NBE on hold at 15% but IMF is urging readiness to tighten

Country-risk blowout via restructuring failure: two failed deal rounds in 2026 (OCC rejection Jan 30, collapse May 31) show execution risk; if the 29 Jun 2026 deal fails comparability-of-treatment or the exchange offer flops, default status persists and spreads stay unquotable

Reformer losing power - NOT close electorally (438-seat landslide, next election ~2031), but a new Ethiopia-Eritrea/Tigray war (Crisis Group 'powder keg') is the realistic path to program derailment via fiscal blowout, FX pressure and donor pullback

Budget flipping back to deficit - partially moot: Ethiopia never ran a surplus; watch that the FY2026/27 deficit widening (2.36% of GDP, spending +21%) stays domestically financeable without reigniting money-printing inflation

access.accessNotes: No ETF, no ADRs; the Ethiopian Securities Exchange only launched in 2025 with no foreign-accessible US instruments. No US retail equity access at all.

reformer.name: John Dramani Mahama (President) with Dr. Cassiel Ato Forson (Finance Minister) as operational driver
reformer.role: President since 7 Jan 2025 (NDC); Ato Forson is Finance Minister running the 'economic reset' program
reformer.nextElection: December 2028 general election; Mahama is constitutionally term-limited and has publicly ruled out running (Aug 2025), so the program faces a succession handover
reformer.notes: Orthodox IMF-anchored consolidation, not Milei-style shock therapy, but unusually credible delivery: statutory primary-surplus floor of 1.5% of GDP written into law, 2025 primary surplus outturn of 2.6% of GDP (vs 1.5% target), overall deficit cut to 1.0% of GDP, public debt down GH¢82.1bn in 2025, VAT overhaul in the 2026 budget (standard rate 21.9%->20%, registration threshold raised to GH¢750k), and a requested 36-month non-financing IMF PCI to anchor policy after the ECF ends. Political backing is strong: NDC won 183-185 of 276 parliamentary seats in Dec 2024. Deductions: heavy tailwind from record gold prices/GoldBod FX flows does part of the work; IMF says structural reforms were 'implemented with delays'; a forex-bureau premium over the interbank rate suggests FX management rather than a fully clean float; and Mahama cannot run in 2028, creating continuity risk (he has pledged not to relax fiscal discipline even in election year 2028).

parallelFx.notes: Ghana runs a managed float, not a formal dual-rate regime, but a persistent and widening retail premium exists: on 3 Jul 2026 the BoG interbank rate was GHS 11.38/11.40 (buy/sell) vs forex bureau rates of GHS 11.80/12.25 - a ~7.5% selling-rate premium. On 1 Jul 2026 bureaus sold at 12.35 vs 11.36 interbank (~8.7% gap). Citi Newsroom flagged the widening interbank-vs-bureau gap from early Feb 2026 (10.95 vs 12.10, ~10%). The cedi appreciated ~40.7% in 2025 (Africa's best performer, first annual gain since 1994) helped by GoldBod gold-for-cedis reserve accumulation, so the premium reflects rationed access at the interbank rate rather than a collapsing peg - but it is the single clearest crack in the story. Trading Economics quoted USD/GHS 11.3762 on 7 Jul 2026. [Verifier: gap narrowed to ~7.5% by 4-6 Jul 2026 (bureau 12.25 vs interbank 11.40).]

countryRisk.rating: Fitch B / Positive (upgraded from B- on 8 May 2026); S&P B-/B / Stable (upgraded from CCC+/C in Nov 2025); Moody's Caa1 / Positive (outlook raised from stable 14 Apr 2026)
countryRisk.notes: Could not verify a current EMBI/USD-bond spread in bps from fetched sources - spread left null rather than estimated. Direction is unambiguous: three positive rating actions in ~8 months; restructured domestic yields fell from ~28% to ~14%; S&P Ghana Sovereign Bond Index 1-yr return was 87.8% as of 20 Feb 2026; Ghana returned to 7-yr domestic issuance (Mar 30-Apr 1, 2026), first in three years. Caveat: a BU GDP Center study (23 Mar 2026) listed Ghana among 12 countries with BOTH rising bond spreads and above-median 2026 debt payments after the late-Feb 2026 oil price shock - a temporary widening within an improving trend. Eurobond restructuring (~US$13bn) done; bilateral deals reached with about half of official creditors under the G20 Common Framework.

GHA.imfProgram: Active/completing: US$3bn, 39-month ECF approved May 2023. Fifth review completed by Executive Board 17 Dec 2025, disbursing ~US$385m (total ~US$2.8bn). Staff-level agreement on the sixth and FINAL review reached 15 May 2026 alongside the 2026 Article IV; final tranche of ~US$360m pending Board approval (not yet confirmed as of 7 Jul 2026), which would bring total disbursements to ~US$3bn. Authorities requested a successor 36-month NON-FINANCING Policy Coordination Instrument (PCI) to anchor policy post-ECF - a signal of graduation rather than dependence. [Verifier: final tranche US$318m per IMF mission chief, Board approval expected ~27 Jul 2026.]

latestInflation.notes: June 2026 CPI rose 5.3% YoY (m/m +0.2%), up from 3.7% in May and the THIRD consecutive monthly rise, driven by non-food (6.3%) - transport, housing, education. Still dramatically below June 2025 (13.7%) and below BoG's 6-10% medium-term band. The trough was 3.2-3.3% in Feb-Mar 2026 (lowest since 1999) after 13 straight months of disinflation from ~23.8% end-2024. Watch: BoG cut 400bps in H1 2026 (18%->15.5% Jan 28; ->14% Mar 18) then held at 14% in May as pressure rebuilt.

GHA.fiscalNote: 2025 outturn (reported Feb 2026): primary SURPLUS of 2.6% of GDP on commitment basis (vs 1.5% program target) and overall deficit of just 1.0% of GDP (vs 2.8% target; ~6-7% deficits in 2024) - IMF confirmed the primary surplus overperformed the program target. Public debt fell GH¢82.1bn in 2025 (GH¢726.7bn -> GH¢630.2bn per the 2026 budget), a ~21ppt debt/GDP drop per Fitch, with debt projected at 46% of GDP by 2027. A primary surplus of at least 1.5% of GDP is now a statutory requirement; the 2026 budget targets continued consolidation (overall deficit projected ~2.6% of GDP in 2026) funded by VAT simplification and spending caps. Trajectory: consolidation holding, best fiscal position in over a decade.
developments: IMF staff completed the 2026 Article IV and reached staff-level agreement on the sixth and FINAL ECF review plus a 36-month non-financing PCI request (mission Apr 29-May 15, 2026); IMF cited primary surplus overperformance, rapid disinflation, rebuilt reserves and sharply lower debt [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/05/15/pr26152-ghana-imf-staff-completes-2026-aiv-consult-reaches-sla-6th-rev-ecf-arr-36mo-pci-request]

Fitch upgraded Ghana to 'B' from 'B-' with Positive outlook on 8 May 2026, citing a 21ppt debt/GDP drop in 2025, unencumbered reserves up $5.4bn to $12.3bn, a current account surplus of 8.2% of GDP in 2025, and inflation of 3.2% in March (lowest since 1999) [source: https://www.gbcghanaonline.com/news/business/fitch-ghana-ato/2026/]

Moody's raised Ghana's outlook to positive from stable on 14 Apr 2026 while affirming Caa1; S&P had upgraded to B-/B (stable) from CCC+/C in Nov 2025 [source: https://tradingeconomics.com/ghana/rating]

Bank of Ghana cut the policy rate from 18% to 15.5% (Jan 28, 2026) and to 14% (Mar 18, 2026) - the March cut on a split 5-1 vote - then paused at 14% in May 2026 after five consecutive cuts as inflation began rebounding [source: https://gna.org.gh/2026/03/bog-cuts-policy-rate-to-14-per-cent-despite-geopolitical-tensions/]

Inflation re-accelerated to 5.3% YoY in June 2026 (from 3.7% in May, 3.2-3.3% trough in Feb-Mar), the third consecutive monthly rise, led by non-food and locally produced items [source: https://www.citinewsroom.com/2026/07/june-inflation-rises-to-5-3-driven-by-higher-non-food-prices/]

Government reported the 2025 fiscal turnaround in Feb 2026: primary surplus 2.6% of GDP, overall deficit 1.0% of GDP, public debt down GH¢82.1bn [source: https://thebftonline.com/2026/02/23/govt-reports-strong-2025-fiscal-turnaround-as-debt-falls-by-gh%C2%A282-1bn-and-inflation-drops-to-3-8/]

Ghana returned to medium/long-term domestic borrowing with its first 7-year bond in three years (Mar 30-Apr 1, 2026), part of a GHS 20.2bn 7-10yr issuance strategy, after restructured yields fell from ~28% to ~14% [source: https://www.africanleadershipmagazine.co.uk/ghana-rebuilds-sovereign-yield-curve-with-2026-bond/]

The gap between the BoG interbank rate and forex bureau rates widened through H1 2026 - bureaus selling USD at GHS 12.25-12.35 vs ~11.36-11.40 interbank in early July (~7.5-8.7% premium), flagged since early February [source: https://citinewsroom.com/2026/02/cedi-under-pressure-widening-gap-between-interbank-and-bureau-rates-marks-start-of-february-2026/]
killCriteriaWatch: Parallel gap reopening - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING: forex-bureau USD selling rates (GHS 12.25-12.35) run ~7.5-9% above the BoG interbank rate (~11.36-11.40) and the gap has widened since Feb 2026, suggesting the interbank rate is below market-clearing and the 2025 cedi appreciation is partly administered via gold-flow surrender rather than a clean float

Inflation momentum turning - WATCH: three consecutive monthly YoY rises to 5.3% in June 2026 after BoG cut 400bps; still below the 6-10% band, but if it re-enters and breaches the band the disinflation leg of the thesis weakens and BoG may have eased too fast

Program anchor weakening - WATCH: the $3bn ECF ends mid-2026 and the successor PCI is non-financing; IMF already notes structural reforms 'implemented with delays'; discipline is untested without Fund money on the line

Reformer transition - MEDIUM-TERM: Mahama is term-limited and has ruled out running in Dec 2028; no anointed successor, so reform continuity past 2028 is unhedged (though not close to tripping today given the 183-185/276 seat majority)
Budget flipping back to deficit - NOT TRIPPING: 2025 overperformed (primary +2.6% GDP) and a 1.5% primary surplus is now statutory; risk rises only into the 2028 election cycle
Country risk blowout - NOT TRIPPING: three positive rating actions in 8 months; only wobble was the late-Feb 2026 oil-shock spread widening that hit petroleum importers including Ghana

reformer.name: William Ruto (President), with John Mbadi (Treasury Cabinet Secretary)
reformer.role: President drives the program; Mbadi (ODM, in cabinet since Aug 2024) runs fiscal policy
reformer.notes: Orthodox, IMF-aligned direction but heavily constrained - nothing like shock therapy. Real wins: Moody's/S&P upgrades, return to Eurobond market (Feb 2026, $2.25bn), reserves rebuilt, single-digit inflation. But the deficit-killing leg keeps slipping: prior $3.6bn IMF program expired April 2025 with ~$850m undisbursed after missed revenue/deficit targets (ninth review abandoned March 2025); FY25/26 deficit was revised UP to ~6% of GDP on a KSh115bn revenue shortfall; Finance Act 2026 (signed June 23, 2026) was deliberately modest ('no new taxes') because the 2024 Gen Z protests broke the government's tax-raising capacity. The currency-freeing leg is arguably going backwards: CBK holds USD/KES pinned at ~129 via heavy dollar sales, which the IMF has flagged as 'excessively stable'. Ruto is unpopular but favored to win 2027 against a divided opposition; deadly protests (127 killed across 2024-25 waves per police watchdog figures cited in press) are the main political risk. Score 38: credible orthodox intent, weak execution and mandate, managed FX contradicts the thesis.

parallelFx.notes: No organized parallel/black market; USD/KES ~129.23-129.30 on 2026-07-07 (Trading Economics/CBK interbank). BUT the rate is de facto managed, not free: it has sat in a ~129.2-129.9 band for ~16 months with 12m std dev ~0.45; CBK Governor Thugge confirmed ~$941m of reserves spent in the four weeks to April 2, 2026 holding 129 (reserves fell $14.6bn Mar 12 -> $13.3bn Apr 9); IMF publicly flagged the shilling as 'excessively stable'. Treasury claimed (Nov 2025) the shilling is UNDERVALUED and would strengthen to ~118 if freed, while Oxford Economics, Standard Chartered and Citigroup see a managed slide to ~134 by end-2026. So gap is 0 today, but the setup resembles a soft peg with step-devaluation risk rather than a clean float.

countryRisk.rating: Moody's B3 stable (upgraded from Caa1, Jan 27, 2026); S&P B stable (upgraded from B-, Aug 22, 2025); Fitch B- stable (affirmed Jan 23, 2026)
countryRisk.notes: No explicit EMBI/spread-in-bps figure found in fetched sources, so spread_bps is null. Sourced yield levels: KENINT 2027 6.115%, KENINT 2032 7.592%, KENINT 2034 7.867% (week ended Jul 3, 2026, Mwango Capital) - versus ~18% peak yields in Nov 2023 and 8.9% in Mar 2025, i.e. a massive multi-year re-rating. Kenya re-entered the Eurobond market Feb 2026 with $2.25bn ($900m 7.875% due 2034 at ~8.1% reoffer; $1.35bn 8.70% due 2039 at ~8.95%), using ~$500m to tender 2028/2032 notes. Near-term wobble: Eurobond yields rose ~14-21bps on average in the week ending Jul 3, 2026 amid protest anniversary and post-budget jitters. Moody's core caveat: debt affordability among weakest in its sovereign universe, interest >30% of revenue.

KEN.imfProgram: None currently in place. The 2021 $3.6bn EFF/ECF (plus RSF) expired April 2025 with the ninth review abandoned (March 2025) and ~$850m undisbursed after Kenya missed deficit/revenue targets. A new funded program has been under negotiation through 2026 (missions Jan and Feb 24-Mar 4, 2026 yielded no lending deal); Mbadi said (Bloomberg, May 11, 2026) Kenya targets agreement by June/July 2026 - possibly ~KSh100bn (~$774m) - accelerated by Iran-war commodity shocks. No staff-level agreement or board approval confirmed as of 2026-07-07; analysts (Bloomberg, Sep 2025) warned talks could drag past the Aug 2027 elections.

latestInflation.notes: June 2026: 6.4% YoY (eased from 6.7% in May), +0.2% m/m; CPI index 154.56 -> 154.91. Still inside CBK's 2.5-7.5% target band but in the upper half and well above 2025 levels - the trajectory over the past year is upward (drivers: food & non-alcoholic beverages +8.6% YoY, transport +16.1% YoY). June PMI showed input-price inflation at its highest since Nov 2023 (fuel/transport, Iran-war passthrough). CBK held the policy rate at 8.75% on June 9, 2026 (second straight hold) and tied the next move to inflation data. Not a hyperinflation-turnaround setup: Kenya is a moderate-inflation country, with inflation currently drifting up, not crashing down.

KEN.fiscalNote: Persistent deficit, consolidation repeatedly slipping - never near surplus. FY2025/26: revenue shortfalls (KSh115.3bn below target by Dec 2025) forced a supplementary budget raising spending to KSh4.533trn and widening the deficit to ~KSh1.14trn ≈ 6% of GDP (vs ~4.8-5.1% originally planned). FY2026/27 budget (presented by Mbadi June 11, 2026): KSh4.82trn expenditure vs KSh3.631trn revenue, deficit KSh1.15trn = 5.5% of GDP, financed mostly domestically (4.8% of GDP domestic vs 0.6% external); Treasury projects narrowing to 3.3% by FY2028/29 - but its past multi-year targets have consistently been missed. Public debt ~68.8% of GDP and rising; interest absorbs >30% of revenue (Moody's). 2026 growth forecast cut to 5.0% from 5.3% on Middle East conflict effects.
developments: Moody's upgraded Kenya from Caa1 to B3 (stable) on Jan 27, 2026, citing improved external liquidity, reserves and market re-access; Fitch affirmed B- (stable) Jan 23, 2026 [source: https://mwangocapital.substack.com/p/02-02-2026-moodys-upgrades-kenya]

Kenya returned to the Eurobond market in Feb 2026 with a $2.25bn dual-tranche deal ($900m 7.875% due 2034, $1.35bn 8.70% due 2039), tendering ~$500m of 2028/2032 notes and pushing out the maturity wall [source: https://mwangocapital.substack.com/p/23-02-2026-kenya-re-enters-the-eurobond-market]

CBK spent ~$941m of reserves in the four weeks to April 2, 2026 holding USD/KES at ~129; reserves fell $14.6bn -> $13.3bn and Wall Street desks (Oxford Economics, StanChart, Citi) forecast a slide to ~134 by end-2026 [source: https://tech-ish.com/2026/04/21/wall-street-kenya-shilling-vulnerable-warning/]

Treasury CS Mbadi said Kenya targets a new funded IMF program agreement by June/July 2026, talks accelerated by Iran-war shocks; no SLA confirmed as of July 7, 2026 [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-11/kenya-targets-new-imf-deal-by-july-as-iran-war-risks-mount]

FY2026/27 budget presented June 11, 2026: KSh4.82trn spending, 5.5%-of-GDP deficit, 2026 growth forecast cut to 5.0% on Middle East conflict; debt seen at 68.8% of GDP [source: https://www.tuko.co.ke/politics/629248-budget-reading-2026-john-mbadi-presens-second-budget-highlighs-national-treasury-cs/]

Ruto signed the Finance Act 2026 on June 23, 2026, insisting 'no new taxes' after the 2024/25 protest backlash - a revenue-cautious bill that protects social peace at the cost of consolidation ambition [source: https://peopledaily.digital/news/ruto-signs-finance-bill-2026-into-law]

CBK MPC held the policy rate at 8.75% on June 9, 2026 (second straight hold) after May inflation jumped to 6.7%, its most contested decision in a year [source: https://kenyanwallstreet.com/mpc-june-2026-hold-hike-data]

Saba Saba anniversary protests on July 7, 2026: Nairobi under heavy police presence, roads to Parliament barricaded; ~127 killed across the 2024-25 protest waves keeps political risk elevated into the 2027 race [source: https://citizen.digital/article/saba-saba-protests-heavy-police-presence-as-roads-closed-in-nairobi-n385970]
killCriteriaWatch: Forced devaluation / parallel gap reopening - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING: the shilling is de facto pegged at ~129 and defended with ~$941m of reserves in a single month (to Apr 2, 2026); IMF calls it 'excessively stable'; sell-side consensus sees ~134 by end-2026. A step-devaluation or messy exit from the soft peg is the single most likely near-term trigger.

Budget flipping back to deficit - effectively already failed/moot: Kenya never achieved balance; FY25/26 deficit widened to ~6% of GDP versus plan on revenue shortfalls, and the Finance Act 2026 deliberately avoids new taxes, so FY26/27's 5.5% target carries downside risk.

Reformer losing power - WATCH, not tripped: Ruto is favored for Aug 2027 against a divided opposition, but is deeply unpopular; deadly Gen Z protest waves (127 killed 2024-25, fresh Saba Saba mobilization July 7, 2026) and warnings of electoral violence make the mandate fragile, and IMF talks could slip past the election.

Country risk blowing out - NOT tripping: ratings upgraded (Moody's B3, S&P B), market access restored at ~8.1-8.95%, yields near multi-year lows (6.1-7.9%); only a modest ~14-21bps backup in the week to Jul 3, 2026.

reformer.name: Anura Kumara Dissanayake (with Deputy Finance Minister/CBSL executing; Dissanayake holds the Finance portfolio)
reformer.role: President and Minister of Finance, NPP/JVP government
reformer.nextElection: Presidential expected ~Sep-Oct 2029 (5-year term from 21 Sep 2024 win; early call constitutionally possible after 4 years); parliament due by 21 Feb 2030; no national election imminent
reformer.notes: Left-populist who campaigned to renegotiate the IMF deal but has governed orthodox: kept the EFF, over-performed the primary-surplus target (~3.8% of GDP in 2025 vs 2.3% target), met all end-Dec-2025 IMF quantitative performance criteria, completed the $12.55bn ISB restructuring exit. Holds a 159/225 parliamentary supermajority. Not Milei-grade shock therapy - gradualist revenue-based consolidation under IMF tutelage, with 2026 fiscal easing (cyclone + fuel subsidies) and eroding popularity (NPP vote share fell from 61.6% in Nov 2024 to 43.3% in May 2025 local elections); strikes/protests handled with essential-services orders. Verified in office as of 1 Jul 2026 (Presidential Secretariat investor event).

parallelFx.notes: No evidence of a meaningful parallel/black market as of Jul 2026; FX market has been unified since the Mar 2023 float. Official/interbank USD/LKR ~334.63 (6 Jul 2026, TradingEconomics; EconomyNext quotes spot 334.75/85). LKR down ~11.2% y/y under Middle East-war oil-shock pressure. Warning sign: a former analyst alleged (May 2026) CBSL is informally instructing banks/money changers to hold USD/LKR in a 329-335 band, and the IMF pointedly said exchange-rate flexibility is 'paramount'/'non-negotiable' (30 Jun 2026) - an enforced cap under sustained BoP pressure is how a parallel gap would reopen. No kerb premium quantifiable from fetched sources.

countryRisk.rating: Fitch CCC+ (upgraded from RD 20 Dec 2024, affirmed 1 Oct 2025); Moody's Caa1 stable (upgraded from Ca, Dec 2024); S&P CCC+/C stable (upgraded from SD 19 Sep 2025)
countryRisk.notes: USD sovereign spread in bps not verifiable from fetched sources (post-restructuring macro-linked/governance-linked bonds; data pages returned placeholders) - null. Direction: risk fell through 2024-25 (three post-default upgrades, restructuring completed Dec 2024 at ~96% participation, 2026 ISB maturities cut from $1,000mn to $330mn), but 2026 Middle East war has tilted risks up: IMF says outlook 'significantly worsened', reserves accumulation decelerating (US$7bn end-Mar 2026 vs ~$14.2bn program-end target), inflation at the CBSL band ceiling. Small positive: modest foreign buying of LKR bonds amid a stabilizing rupee in early Jul 2026.

LKA.imfProgram: 4-year EFF of SDR 2.3bn (~US$3bn) approved 20 Mar 2023, runs to ~Mar 2027. Combined 5th+6th reviews completed by the Executive Board on 27 May 2026 (5th review was deferred after Cyclone Ditwah), releasing SDR 508mn (~US$695mn). All end-Dec-2025 QPCs met; most structural benchmarks met or done with delay; one PC waiver granted. Separate US$206mn Rapid Financing Instrument approved 19 Dec 2025 for cyclone relief. IMF staff visit 24-30 Jun 2026: 2026 growth cut to ~3% (from 5% in 2025) on the Middle East war, temporary fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies tolerated but primary surplus must return to 2.3% of GDP in 2027; 7th review mission in fall 2026. No successor-arrangement announcement found in fetched sources (null).

latestInflation.notes: CCPI (CBSL release 30 Jun 2026): headline 6.8% y/y in Jun 2026 (vs 5.5% May, 1.6% Feb) - a three-year high approaching the top of CBSL's 5%±2pp band; +2.1% m/m; core 4.0%, non-food 8.4%, food 3.6%. Driver is energy-price pass-through from the Middle East war (Hormuz disruption; Sri Lanka imports ~2/3 of energy), not domestic money-printing. CBSL hiked policy rate 100bp in response. Disinflation achievement of 2023-25 (deflation through much of 2025, 2.2% in Mar 2026) is being tested by an exogenous shock.

LKA.fiscalNote: 2025 outturn: overall deficit ~4.5% of GDP with primary surplus ~3.8% of GDP - well above the IMF's 2.3% floor; all end-Dec-2025 fiscal QPCs met. 2026 budget (presented 7 Nov 2025, explicitly IMF-aligned) targets 2.5% primary surplus, 15.4% of GDP revenue, 5.1% overall deficit; but Cyclone Ditwah (Rs 500bn supplementary allocation) plus Middle East-war fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies mean acknowledged fiscal easing in 2026, with a stated commitment (endorsed by IMF, 30 Jun 2026) to return to the 2.3% primary-surplus target in 2027. Primary balance remains in surplus; trajectory temporarily loosening, not flipping.
developments: Cyclone Ditwah (28 Nov 2025) killed 600+, displaced 100k+; IMF approved US$206mn RFI emergency financing on 19 Dec 2025 and deferred the 5th EFF review; parliament rushed a Rs 500bn supplementary budget [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2025/12/19/pr-25436-sri-lanka-imf-approves-us-206-million-in-emergency-financial-support]

Middle East war/Strait of Hormuz disruption in 2026 hit the two-thirds-imported energy bill: fuel rationing and a four-day work week for state institutions imposed; sustained ~$120 oil would swell the fuel import bill to $4.5-5bn and risk a BoP crisis [source: https://www.npr.org/2026/06/02/nx-s1-5823430/global-conflicts-push-sri-lankas-economy-to-the-brink]

Inflation surged from 1.6% y/y (Feb 2026) to 6.8% y/y (Jun 2026) on energy pass-through, nearing CBSL's 7% band ceiling; CBSL hiked 100bp and deployed macroprudential measures [source: https://www.cbsl.gov.lk/en/news/ccpi-inflation-june-2026]

IMF Executive Board completed combined 5th+6th EFF reviews on 27 May 2026 (~US$695mn disbursed); all end-Dec-2025 QPCs met, 2025 growth 5%, reserves $7bn end-Mar 2026, but 2026 growth cut to ~3% on the war [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/05/27/pr26172-sri-lanka-imf-completes-combined-5th-and-6th-reviews-under-eff]

IMF staff visit (24-30 Jun 2026) blessed temporary subsidies but demanded return to 2.3% primary surplus in 2027, cost-recovery energy pricing, and warned exchange-rate flexibility is 'paramount' - 7th review mission in fall 2026 [source: https://www.cbsl.gov.lk/en/news/IMF-staff-concludes-visit-to-sri-lanka-20260630]

Allegation (May 2026) that CBSL is unofficially capping USD/LKR at 329-335 via moral suasion on banks and money changers as the rupee fell ~11% y/y - the key crack to watch in the floated-FX story [source: https://www.lankaweb.com/news/items/2026/05/24/central-bank-unofficially-caps-dollar-rate-to-curb-rupee-depreciation-says-rajith-keerthi-thennakoon/]

2026 budget (7 Nov 2025), themed on fiscal discipline, targets 2.5% primary surplus and 5.1% deficit with 15.4% revenue/GDP, drawing cautious praise from Fitch; 2025 deficit out-turn ~4.5% [source: https://economynext.com/sri-lanka-2026-budget-targets-deficit-of-5-1-pct-of-gdp-2025-out-turn-4-5-pct-247288/]

S&P raised Sri Lanka's foreign-currency rating to CCC+/C stable on 19 Sep 2025 after the SriLankan Airlines guaranteed-bond restructuring - completing the exit from default status at all three agencies; NPP's May 2025 local-election vote share fell to 43.3% (from 61.6%), signaling austerity fatigue [source: https://economynext.com/sp-raises-sri-lankas-rating-from-selective-default-on-stable-outlook-241296/]
killCriteriaWatch: FORCED DEVAL / PARALLEL GAP REOPENING - CLOSEST TO TRIPPING: LKR -11% y/y, reserves accumulation stalling ($7bn vs ~$14.2bn program-end target), credible allegation of an unofficial CBSL cap at 329-335, and explicit IMF pushback that FX flexibility is 'non-negotiable'. If the cap hardens while oil stays high, a kerb premium and import backlog would reopen the 2022 playbook.

INFLATION / COUNTRY RISK - WATCH: CPI 6.8% is at the top of CBSL's 5±2% band and rising (2.1% m/m in June); exogenous (oil) not monetary, and CBSL hiked 100bp, but a second-round wage-price spiral or sustained ~$120 oil would blow out the current account and re-rate risk wider. No verified spread series to monitor - get Bloomberg/EMBI access.

FISCAL SLIPPAGE - PARTIAL/WATCH: 2026 is an acknowledged easing year (cyclone reconstruction + fuel/electricity/fertilizer subsidies) against a 2.5% primary-surplus budget target; the tell is the fall-2026 7th review - if the 2027 return to a 2.3% primary surplus slips or energy pricing abandons cost-recovery, the fiscal leg of the thesis breaks. Primary balance still in surplus today.

REFORMER LOSING POWER - NOT CLOSE MECHANICALLY, ERODING POLITICALLY: supermajority runs to ~2029/2030 with no imminent election, but vote share collapsed ~18pp within 8 months of taking office (May 2025 locals), and fuel rationing/strike suppression is stoking unrest; the risk is not losing office but buying popularity with IMF-breaking giveaways.

access.accessNotes: No US-listed ETF and zero US-exchange ADRs (a few London GDRs only); the frontier wrapper FM was itself liquidated Jan 2025. Effectively no US retail equity access - USD sovereign bonds via EM-debt funds are the realistic route.

reformer.name: Bola Tinubu (President), with Taiwo Oyedele (Finance Minister since 21 Apr 2026, architect of the 2025 tax reform) and Yemi Cardoso (CBN Governor)
reformer.role: President driving the program; technocrat finance minister and orthodox central bank executing it
reformer.nextElection: Presidential/National Assembly election moved up to 16 January 2027 (INEC revised timetable Feb 2026, from Feb 20); campaigns start 19 Aug 2026
reformer.notes: Three years of sustained orthodox reform since May 2023: fuel subsidy removal, FX unification/float, tight money (MPR 26.5%), 2025 tax overhaul effective 1 Jan 2026. Tinubu secured APC endorsement for 2027 (May 2026) and doubled down publicly on reforms. April 2026 reshuffle REPLACED reform-credible Edun with the even more reform-identified tax czar Oyedele - signal read as continuity/tightening of the economic team, though Semafor reporting suggests patronage friction (Chagoury road-project funding) contributed to Edun's exit. Not Milei-grade: the 2026 election-year budget is expansionary (deficit widened, borrowing plan raised 63%), poverty ~60-63% fuels backlash, and IMF found ~2% of GDP unreported spending. Political risk currently muted: the opposition ADC coalition fractured in May 2026 (Obi and Kwankwaso quit for the NDC; Atiku stayed in a leadership-crisis-ridden ADC), leaving Tinubu favored but reliant on continued spending. Score 65 = serious, credentialed, durable orthodox program, but gradualist and fiscally slipping into the election, not shock therapy.

parallelFx.notes: Naira was unified/floated in June 2023; the pre-2023 gap (>60%) is essentially closed. Same-day comparison 30 Jun 2026: official/CBN ~N1,381/USD vs street sell N1,400 (buy N1,390) = ~1.4% gap. Other reads: 27 Jun official N1,378.47 vs parallel ~N1,400 (~1.6%); 6 Jul parallel N1,400-1,410 (30-day range N1,395-1,415), implying a ~1-2.5% premium. Functionally unified. Naira APPRECIATED through H1-2026 (from ~N1,4xx to ~N1,376-1,381 official) as reserves crossed $50bn; CBN reportedly managing the pace of appreciation. [Verifier: as of 6-7 Jul 2026 official ~N1,370 vs parallel N1,400-1,415 -> ~2.2-3.3% gap, slightly wider than the 30-Jun 1.4% snapshot; "functionally unified" still holds.]

countryRisk.rating: S&P B/stable (upgraded from B- on 15 May 2026 - first upgrade in 14 years); Fitch B/stable (upgraded from B- 11 Apr 2025, affirmed 15 Apr 2026); Moody's B3/stable (upgraded from Caa1 30 May 2025)
countryRisk.notes: Could not fetch a clean EMBI/T-spread print (Cbonds and Businessday blocked fetches) - spread_bps left null rather than estimated. Verified proxies: average Nigeria sovereign eurobond yield fell to 6.78% in early June 2026, the YTD low; Businessday reports the spread over US Treasuries compressed ~80bps to 'levels not seen in over a decade'; yields briefly spiked to 7.43% (+26bps) in June during the Middle East/Iran flare-up, then re-rallied. All three agencies upgraded within 13 months on FX liberalization, subsidy removal, reserves ($50bn March 2026 vs $33bn 2023 per S&P) and debt/revenue down to 338% from 500%. DMO is appointing advisers for a fresh eurobond to refinance expensive debt - market access clearly open. Trend: falling/compressing.

NGA.imfProgram: None - surveillance only. 2026 Article IV consultation concluded by the Executive Board on 1 June 2026 (PR 26/190, published 9 June 2026). No lending arrangement; then-Finance Minister Edun explicitly ruled out an IMF borrowing program in April 2026. IMF assessment: growth 4.0% (2025 est.) and 4.1% (2026 proj.); called for a NEUTRAL 2026 fiscal stance while the actual budget is expansionary; flagged ~2% of GDP of public spending omitted from official budgets; welcomed tax reforms and FATF grey-list exit.

latestInflation.notes: Huge disinflation year-on-year (26.27% in Feb 2025; ~30%+ peak in 2024 on the old base) but the YoY rate has now RISEN three consecutive months: 15.06% Feb -> 15.38% Mar -> 15.69% Apr -> 15.93% May 2026. M/m slowed to 1.75% in May from 2.13% in April - still annualizing ~20%+. Food is the biggest contributor (6.38pp of the YoY basket). Fitch expects ~16% average in 2026. CBN cut the MPR once (27%->26.5%, Feb 2026) then held at 26.5% in May 2026; disinflation is stalling, not reversing violently.

NGA.fiscalNote: Still in deficit and widening into the election - the weakest pillar of the thesis. IMF: consolidated government deficit rose to ~4.4% of GDP in 2025; the 2026 budget implies an FGN deficit of ~4.4% of GDP (vs 3.5% financed in 2025) and an EXPANSIONARY stance, against IMF advice of neutral. The 2026 budget was expanded to N68.32tn spending vs N36.87tn revenue, a N31.46tn deficit (deficit ~70-85% of revenue), with the borrowing plan raised 63% to N29.2tn and up to ~$10bn of new external debt in the 2026 pipeline. IMF also flagged ~2% of GDP of unreported spending, so true deficits are larger than headline. Offsets: revenue mobilization is genuinely improving (tax collections N12.3tn 2023 -> N28.3tn 2025; N21.6tn in H1-2026), debt/GDP ~32-35% and debt service burden falling relative to revenue.
developments: Tax reform went live: the Nigeria Tax Act and Tax Administration Act took effect 1 Jan 2026 - ~97-98% of wage earners pay less or no PAYE and ~97% of small businesses exempt from CIT/VAT/WHT; two administration acts (revenue service, joint board) already operating since June 2025 [source: https://punchng.com/fg-confirms-major-tax-reforms-to-take-effect-january-1-2026/]

CBN delivered its first rate cut of the cycle in Feb 2026 (MPR 27% -> 26.5%, 304th MPC) and held at 26.5% in May 2026 as inflation ticked back up [source: https://financeinafrica.com/insights/cbn-cuts-benchmark-interest-rate/]

FX reserves crossed $50bn in March 2026 - a ~13-year high, up from $33bn in 2023 - reaching $50.96bn by 17 June 2026 (~9.7 months import cover); the naira appreciated to ~N1,376-1,381/USD official with the parallel gap at ~1-2% [source: https://dailypost.ng/2026/03/12/naira-records-highest-appreciation-against-us-dollar-as-foreign-reserves-hit-50-01bn/]

S&P upgraded Nigeria to B/stable on 15 May 2026 - its first upgrade in 14 years - completing a three-agency upgrade sweep after Fitch (B, Apr 2025; affirmed Apr 2026) and Moody's (B3, May 2025) [source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-15/s-p-raises-nigeria-s-rating-for-first-time-in-14-years]

Surprise cabinet reshuffle 21 Apr 2026: tax-reform architect Taiwo Oyedele replaced Wale Edun as Finance Minister & Coordinating Minister of the Economy - read as reform continuity/doubling-down, though reporting cites insider friction over project funding releases [source: https://www.semafor.com/article/04/22/2026/nigerias-tinubu-replaces-finance-minister-in-surprise-reshuffle]

IMF concluded the 2026 Article IV on 1 June 2026: growth 4.0% (2025)/4.1% (2026), reforms praised, but IMF urged a neutral 2026 fiscal stance and separately disclosed ~2% of GDP of spending omitted from official budgets [source: https://www.imf.org/en/news/articles/2026/06/09/pr26190-nigeria-imf-executive-board-concludes-2026-article-iv-consultation-with-nigeria]

Election-year fiscal expansion: 2026 budget grew to N68.32tn with a N31.46tn deficit; the 2026 borrowing plan was raised to N29.2tn (+N11.3tn) and the DMO is appointing advisers for a fresh eurobond, exploiting decade-low spreads (avg eurobond yield 6.78% in June) to refinance costly debt [source: https://www.legit.ng/business-economy/economy/1704230-fg-raises-2026-borrowing-plan-n292tn-budget-deficit-widens/]

Real-economy tailwinds: Nigeria recorded its first-ever net petrol exports in March 2026 (domestic refining ~700kbpd, Dangote effect), oil output hit 1.9mbpd in May 2026 (above OPEC quota), and Q1-2026 GDP grew 3.89% y/y; meanwhile the opposition ADC coalition fractured in May 2026 (Obi and Kwankwaso defected to the NDC), improving Tinubu's re-election odds [source: https://thenewsnigeria.com.ng/2026/07/07/295552/]
killCriteriaWatch: FISCAL (closest to tripping): budget never reached surplus and is deteriorating into the Jan 2027 election - 2026 deficit widened to N31.46tn (~4.4% of GDP, expansionary vs IMF's neutral-stance advice), borrowing plan raised 63% to N29.2tn, and the IMF revealed ~2% of GDP of unreported spending, meaning true deficits exceed headline. This is the primary thesis risk.

INFLATION/DISINFLATION STALL (watch): YoY inflation has risen three consecutive months (15.06% Feb -> 15.93% May 2026) and m/m of 1.75-2.13% annualizes >20%; if election spending plus a softer naira push it back over ~20%, the re-rating narrative breaks.

REFORMER POWER (moderate, rising into Jan 16, 2027): Tinubu is endorsed by APC and the opposition is fragmented (ADC coalition collapsed May 2026), so near-term continuity is favored - but the transmission channel of election risk is the spending spree already visible in the 2026 budget, and campaigns begin Aug 2026.
NOT tripping: parallel-FX gap (~1-2%, effectively unified, naira appreciating on $50bn reserves) and country risk (spreads at decade lows, three rating upgrades in 13 months, market access open).

Nearest proxies: VanEck Africa ETF AFK (active but thin) and Jumia (NYSE: JMIA). Lagos-listed names: effectively no US retail access.
